chunks.page_summary: This page provides a guide on integrating multiple Rheonics SRV and SRD sensors into Ignition SCADA via MQTT, using Node-RED to read data from Rheonics SME devices and publish it with a structured topic naming convention.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863
Integrating Multiple Rheonics Sensors via MQTT in Ignition SCADA
Modified on: Mon, 1 Sep, 2025 at 4:45 PM
What is the purpose of this article?
This article demonstrates how to integrate multiple Rheonics sensors simultaneously in Ignition SCADA via MQTT. This is done by configuring Node-RED to read data from several Rheonics SME.
To learn how to send Rheonics sensor data to an MQTT broker using Node-RED refer to Integrating Rheonics Sensors with Node-RED and MQTT for Remote Data Access
To learn how to connect Ignition SCADA to a MQTT broker to visualize Rheonics sensor data refer to Using Ignition SCADA to Visualize Rheonics Sensor Data via MQTT
1. Overview
In a real-world scenario, remote visualization of multiple sensor data is highly valuable for fluid monitoring and diagnostics across different locations. This section demonstrates how to connect and publish data from several Rheonics sensors through Node-RED, such as multiple SRVs and SRDs, through individual MQTT topics for each parameter.
The following diagram presents the extended architecture used in this multiple sensor configuration.
Figure 1: Architecture with Multiple Sensors
2. Node-RED setup
To integrate various sensors, data is first obtained from each Rheonics SME via Modbus TCP (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/folders/81000217062). In Node-RED, this is done by adding a new Modbus client for each SME, assigning each its respective IP address. In this example, parameters from three SRV sensors and one SRD sensor are read, each configured with its own IP. Each Modbus-Read node in Node-RED is paired with a corresponding MQTT-out node to publish the sensor data.
2.1. MQTT Topic Naming Convention
To keep topics organized, follow a structured naming scheme. Ensure each sensor's data is published to a unique MQTT topic to prevent conflicts and to make data handling easier on the Ignition Designer side. For this example, the format used is the following:
  SRV0
    viscosity: srv0/viscosity
    density: srv0/density
    temperature: srv0/temperature
  SRV1
    viscosity: srv1/viscosity
    density: srv1/density
    temperature: srv1/temperature
  SRV2
    viscosity: srv2/viscosity
    density: srv2/density
    temperature: srv2/temperature
  SRD
    viscosity: srd/viscosity
    density: srd/density
    temperature: srd/temperature
    kinviscosity: srd/kinviscosity
The final Node-RED flow looks as the following image:
key_facts: This article demonstrates how to integrate multiple Rheonics sensors (SRV, SRD) simultaneously in Ignition SCADA via MQTT.
The integration is achieved by configuring Node-RED to read data from several Rheonics SME devices.
Remote visualization of multiple sensor data is highly valuable for fluid monitoring and diagnostics across different locations.
Rheonics sensors publish data through individual MQTT topics for each parameter.
Sensor data is initially obtained from each Rheonics SME via Modbus TCP.
In Node-RED, a new Modbus client must be added for each SME, each assigned its respective IP address.
The example configuration reads parameters from three SRV sensors and one SRD sensor.
Each Modbus-Read node in Node-RED is paired with an MQTT-out node to publish sensor data.
A structured MQTT topic naming scheme is used to organize topics and prevent conflicts for easier data handling in Ignition Designer.
diagram_labels: Rheonics Sensors (eg. SRV, SRD, DVP, others) for density, viscosity, temperature

chunks.page_summary: This page details the configuration of Rheonics sensors within Ignition SCADA using MQTT and Node-RED for data organization and subsequent visualization in Ignition Designer.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 2: Node-RED flow with multiple sensors

3. Configuring Ignition SCADA MQTT
In Ignition SCADA, each SME can be linked to its custom namespace within the MQTT Engine module. This can help organize data by sensor and ensure clear identification across the interface.
*   Name: For this example, each sensor's serial number was used as the namespace name and as the Root Tag Folder name.
*   Subscriptions: Assign the MQTT topics to each namespace based on the naming structure used in Node-RED.

This image displays the MQTT Engine's configuration, showing how each sensor's topics are grouped into their namespace. In this case, the serial name of the sensor was used for the Root Tag Folder, so it is easier to find the tags for each sensor.

Default | Custom

Name | Subscriptions | Root Tag Folder | Tag Name | JSON Payload
A0-0000 | srd/viscosity,srd/temperature,srd/density,srd/kinviscosity | A0-0000 | false | delete edit
A0-0001 | srv1/viscosity,srv1/temperature | A0-0001 | false | delete edit
A0-0002 | srv2/viscosity,srv2/temperature | A0-0002 | false | delete edit
CEBW-B09-109 | srv0/temperature,srv0/viscosity | CEBW-B09-109 | false | delete edit
→ Create new Custom Namespace...

Figure 3: Ignition MQTT Engine Configuration with Multiple Namespaces

4. Visualization in Ignition Designer
Using the Rheonics templates available on Ignition Exchange portal (https://inductiveautomation.com/exchange/), sensor data can be easily visualized. For this example, the existing display template was modified to have more SRV displays available to correctly assign each display to its respective tag.

The following figure shows the Tag Browser in Ignition Designer, where the sensor values are grouped under each namespace created previously, corresponding to each SME.
key_facts: Each SME (Sensor/Module/Equipment) in Ignition SCADA can be linked to a custom namespace within the MQTT Engine module.
Using custom namespaces helps organize sensor data and ensures clear identification across the interface.
Sensor serial numbers are used as both the namespace name and the Root Tag Folder name in the MQTT Engine configuration.
MQTT topics are assigned to each namespace based on the naming structure defined in Node-RED.
The MQTT Engine's configuration groups sensor topics into their respective namespaces, with the sensor's serial name serving as the Root Tag Folder for easy tag retrieval.
Rheonics provides templates on the Ignition Exchange portal (https://inductiveautomation.com/exchange/) for visualizing sensor data.

An existing display template was modified to incorporate more SRV (likely 'sensor reference value' or a specific sensor display type) displays, facilitating correct assignment of displays to their respective tags.
Sensor values are grouped under previously created namespaces in the Tag Browser within Ignition Designer, corresponding to each SME.
diagram_labels: Ignition MQTT Engine Configuration with Multiple Namespaces

chunks.page_summary: This page illustrates how to integrate Rheonics viscometers (SRV) and density meters (SRD) with Ignition Designer using MQTT to display real-time sensor data.

5. References
*   Integrating Rheonics SME with Node-RED and MQTT for Remote Data Access (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000418655)
*   Using Ignition SCADA to Visualize Rheonics SME Data via MQTT (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000217062)
*   Modbus TCP (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/folders/81000217062)
*   Ignition Exchange portal (https://inductiveautomation.com/exchange/)
key_facts: Rheonics sensors (SRV viscometers and SRD density meters) can be integrated with Ignition Designer for data visualization.
Data from Rheonics sensors is accessible via a Tag Browser in Ignition Designer, organized into folders per sensor.
The Ignition Designer visualization displays real-time viscosity, temperature, density, and kinematic viscosity measurements from multiple sensors.
The document provides references for integrating Rheonics SME with Node-RED and MQTT, visualizing SME data via MQTT with Ignition SCADA, Modbus TCP, and the Ignition Exchange portal.
